steps.title: Use Hanke's measure
steps.content: Hanke interprets decreases in total federal outlays relative to GDP as decreases in government size. The underlined claim is about whether a divided Congress may be necessary but not sufficient for that decrease. So the supporting data must focus on total outlays, not only defense or nondefense outlays.

steps.title: Apply the condition logic
steps.content: "Necessary but not sufficient" means two things here. If divided Congress is necessary, decreases in total outlays should occur with divided Congresses rather than undivided ones. If divided Congress is not sufficient, at least one divided Congress should fail to produce a decrease in total outlays.

steps.title: Match the table pattern
steps.content: The supporting pattern must therefore show that divided Congresses usually line up with reductions in total outlays, while also showing an exception. It should also show that undivided Congresses line up with increases in total outlays. That combination supports both halves of the underlined claim about divided Congress being necessary but not sufficient.

steps.title: Reject wrong comparisons
steps.content: Choice A compares nondefense and defense outlays, not total outlays. Choice B says all divided Congresses reduced total outlays, which would make divided Congress sufficient. Choice C again shifts away from total outlays to either nondefense or defense outlays. The choice with all but one divided Congress reducing total outlays and undivided Congresses increasing total outlays fits the claim. The answer is D.
